data.title: SLB outlines $1B data center run rate exit for 2026 amid Middle East disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SLB (SLB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "It was a challenging start of the year, marked by severe disruption in the Middle East that impacted our first quarter revenue and earnings." (CEO & Director Olivier Le Peuch)

  • "Production Systems' year-on-year revenue increased 23% due to the acquisition of ChampionX, which continued to deliver accretive growth." (CEO Le Peuch) "Additionally, we're on track to achieve our synergies target." (CEO Le Peuch)

  • "Revenue increased 9% year-on-year, driven by strong uptake in Digital Operations." (CEO Le Peuch) "Data Center Solutions remains a bright spot with 45% growth year-on-year." (CEO Le Peuch) "With our growing backlog, we remain on track to exit the year at $1 billion run rate and expect the growth rate to accelerate in 2027." (CEO Le Peuch)

  • "First quarter earnings per share, excluding charges and credits, was $0.52." (Executive VP & CFO Stephane Biguet) "Overall, our first quarter global revenue of $8.7 billion increased 3% year-on-year." (CFO Biguet)

Outlook

  • "As a result, it is challenging to provide precise guidance for this quarter." (CEO Le Peuch) "Under this assumption, we estimate that the sequential revenue and earnings decline in the Middle East will be fully offset by all other international markets combined, where we anticipate mid- to high single-digit revenue growth with improved margins." (CEO Le Peuch) "Meanwhile, North America revenue is expected to be flat sequentially." (CEO Le Peuch)

  • "Under the specific scenario that Olivier highlighted earlier... we estimate that it would negatively impact our second quarter earnings per share by an incremental $0.06 to $0.08 when compared to the first quarter." (CFO Biguet)

  • "By division, under the middle scenario... Digital and Production Systems will grow globally, while Reservoir Performance and Well Construction will decline globally." (CEO Le Peuch)

Financial Results

  • "When compared to the fourth quarter of last year, revenue fell by just over $1 billion or 10.5%." (CFO Biguet) "This decline was approximately 200 basis points or about $200 million higher than we expected at the time of our last earnings call in January." (CFO Biguet)

  • "Company-wide adjusted EBITDA margin for the first quarter was 20.3%, down 346 basis points year-on-year." (CFO Biguet) "We also incurred additional logistics and materials costs as a result of supply chain disruptions due to the conflict." (CFO Biguet)

  • "First quarter Digital revenue of $640 million increased 9% year-on-year." (CFO Biguet) "Annual recurring revenue for the division stood at $1.02 billion at the end of the first quarter, representing year-on-year growth of 15%." (CFO Biguet)

  • "Our net debt increased $797 million sequentially to $8.2 billion." (CFO Biguet) "Free cash flow was slightly negative at $23 million on account of the payment of annual employee incentives and the seasonal increase in working capital." (CFO Biguet) "During the quarter, we repurchased $451 million of our stock, and we still expect to repurchase a minimum of $2.4 billion for the full year." (CFO Biguet)

Q&A

  • John Anderson, Barclays: asked whether the 2027-2028 investment uptick depended on structurally higher oil prices and which end markets had the most upside; CEO Le Peuch: "we are projecting that the commodity price will be higher after this than they were before" and cited inventory replenishment and energy security as drivers, adding that "offshore" is "very attractive" and "Subsea, we expect OneSubsea to benefit at scale."

  • James West, Melius Research: asked how Middle East recovery could unfold and what customers need first; CEO Le Peuch: "we are more in standby as we speak" and described a phased recovery where some areas resume "within days or weeks" while others require "well intervention activity."

  • Stephen Richardson, Evercore ISI: asked about the S&P deal and data center momentum; CEO Le Peuch: "we have come to an agreement with S&P Global Energy to acquire actually their upstream petrotechnical software suite, not their data" and said SLB also entered "an agreement to pursue a strategic partnership" using "the global data sets of S&P Global Energy" with Lumi and Tela; on data centers, he reiterated "we will reach or exceed the $1 billion run rate" and highlighted NVIDIA selecting SLB as "their design partner for the DSX AI factory."

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked about Digital margin dynamics; CFO Biguet: "EBITDA margins... were indeed lower... exclusively due to lower amortization" and said it is "clearly our ambition to deliver the total EBITDA margins from Digital of at least 35% this year as well."

  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: asked for specific cost pressure points and ChampionX integration observations; CFO Biguet: "the line item that's the most impacted is logistics and transportation costs" and "raw materials... would also include chemicals," adding SLB is "activating inflation pass-through clauses"; CEO Le Peuch: "ChampionX has been as a portfolio accretive" and said customers were "very pleased with the integration progress."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly negative, concentrating on disruption mechanics and sensitivity points (e.g., Marc Bianchi asking if the message was "results will be the same as first quarter" and Neil Mehta pressing for "specific items" behind cost pressure).

  • Management tone was slightly positive but conditional, repeatedly anchoring on uncertainty while asserting readiness and offsets, including "it is challenging to provide precise guidance" (CEO Le Peuch) alongside "we stand ready" and "we are committed and ready to support our customers" (CEO Le Peuch).

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 language shifted from broad confidence in an improving exit rate to explicit contingency framing tied to the conflict, while maintaining growth narratives in Digital, Production Recovery, and Data Centers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, management centered the quarter on the Middle East shock ("severe disruption") and its financial knock-on effects, while Q4 emphasized "strong operational and financial performance" and a 2026 full-year revenue range of "$36.9 billion to $37.7 billion" (CEO Le Peuch, prior quarter).

  • Q1 guidance language became scenario-based rather than range-based, including "challenging to provide precise guidance" (CEO Le Peuch) and an explicit incremental EPS sensitivity of "$0.06 to $0.08" tied to disruption duration (CFO Biguet).

  • The strategic narrative stayed consistent across calls on ChampionX, Digital/AI, OneSubsea, and Data Centers, but Q1 introduced a new M&A item in Digital (S&P software suite) and elevated the NVIDIA DSX partnership as a proof point for the data center expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • "It is uncertain how long geopolitical disruption will last and how the recovery in the Middle East will unfold." (CEO Le Peuch) "We are facing higher procurement and logistics costs driven by the conflict." (CEO Le Peuch)

  • "We did not make any material adjustment to our cost base during the quarter as our immediate focus was the protection of our people and preserving operational capacity for the expected future rebound in activity." (CFO Biguet) "We have mobilized our commercial organization to recover some of these increased costs." (CFO Biguet)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a conflict-driven reset that pushed revenue and margins below internal expectations, while emphasizing that ChampionX integration, Digital Operations adoption, and Data Center Solutions traction (including the NVIDIA DSX design-partner role and a reiterated $1 billion run-rate exit target) remain central to SLB’s strategy. For Q2, leadership leaned on a defined scenario: Middle East disruption persisting into mid-quarter, an incremental EPS impact of $0.06 to $0.08, and an offset from mid- to high single-digit international growth outside the region, with cost recovery efforts focused on logistics and petroleum-linked inputs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: WSFS outlines 25-35 bps net charge-offs outlook while expanding buyback authorization to 19%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WSFS Financial Corporation (WSFS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "WSFS had a strong start to 2026, continuing to demonstrate the strength of our franchise and diverse business model." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg) "Our first quarter results included a core EPS of $1.68, core ROA of 1.65% and core return on tangible common equity of 20.7%, which are all up versus the prior quarter and prior year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "These results include the previously disclosed loan recovery of $15.7 million." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Excluding this recovery, core EPS was $1.45." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Core results for the first quarter exclude 2 items related to the sales of real estate properties" and "These items resulted in a $2.2 million negative impact to net income and $0.04 impact to EPS." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Net interest margin of 3.83% was flat linked quarter while absorbing the interest rate cuts that occurred in the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "This margin reflects a reduction of 12 basis points in total client deposit costs to 1.33%." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Core fee revenue, which represents nearly 1/3 of total revenue, grew 11% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "This was driven by broad-based growth across our fee businesses and led by Wealth & Trust, which grew 25% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Corporate Trust" and "Global Capital Markets" were "each up over 40% year-over-year as we continue to win new mandates and capture market share." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "During the quarter, we continued to execute on our capital return framework and returned $94 million of capital, including $85 million in buybacks, which equates to 2.5% of our outstanding shares." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Since the beginning of 2025, WSFS has repurchased approximately 12% of our outstanding shares." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "The Board approved an 18% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.20 per share" and "an additional share repurchase authorization of 15% of our outstanding shares as of quarter end" bringing "our total authorization to 19% of outstanding shares." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

Outlook

  • "We updated our annual outlook for net charge-offs as a result of the recovery." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg) "Our new outlook is now 25 to 35 basis points for the year, down from the previous outlook of 35 to 45 basis points." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "As part of our typical process, we will provide an updated full year outlook when we present our 2Q results in July." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Clearly, the March cut didn't happen." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "As you noted, we are asset sensitive, so that does provide a little bit of a tailwind for us." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Generally kind of about 2 basis points per rate cut across the year was the cost to us of the rate cut." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "We are seeing more deposit competition for sure, really across all the businesses." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "We have a number of promotional products out there as we continue to try to grow clients and win market share." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

Financial Results

  • "Net recoveries for the quarter were $3.5 million as the previously disclosed $15.7 million recovery more than offset the charge-offs." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg) "Excluding the impact of this recovery, net charge-offs were $12.2 million." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Client deposits increased 5% linked quarter" and "On a year-over-year basis, our deposits are up over 9%." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Importantly, noninterest deposits grew 14% linked quarter and now represents 34% of our total deposits, up from 29% in the first quarter of last year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "We saw meaningful improvement across delinquencies and problem assets." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Delinquencies are down 32% year-over-year and problem assets are down 26% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg) "Nonperforming assets, which are down 25% year-over-year, increased linked quarter driven by 2 loans" including "a C&I loan and a multifamily loan." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Cash Connect fees declined quarter-over-quarter due to the impact of interest rate cuts and lower volumes, but the business delivered a strong profit margin of 15%, more than doubling its profit margin year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

Q&A

  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.: asked about deposit growth sustainability and how much was market share vs. transactional; Executive VP & CFO David Burg said, "we did have some elevated transactional deposits at the end of the quarter" and "Certainly, would not take this quarter and extrapolate it out in terms of the growth rate for the year," while adding Trust growth reflects "strong growth in the market as well as our ability to take share and grow faster than the market." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.: asked to sensitize the outlook to fewer/no rate cuts; Executive VP & CFO Burg said, "we are asset sensitive" and reiterated, "about 2 basis points per rate cut across the year was the cost to us of the rate cut," while cautioning, "We are seeing more deposit competition" and concluding, "our NIM should be more or less stable." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: asked about muted period-end loan growth vs. strong C&I momentum and the cadence of partnership consumer runoff; Chairman, President & CEO Rodger Levenson said, "the spring portfolio will continue to roll off" and management’s goal is "to offset as much as possible" with home lending, while adding, "C&I should be the primary driver." (Chairman, President & CEO Levenson)

  • Christopher Marinac, Brean Capital: asked whether potential regulatory changes could alter capital plans; Executive VP & CFO David Burg referenced the "multiyear glide path" and said preliminary modeling suggests "maybe a 4% to 5% benefit to capital" from rule changes, while emphasizing they monitor "multiple capital ratios." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked how to think about Institutional Services growth durability; Executive VP & CFO Burg said the company has been "investing in headcount and technology" and sees "strong market growth" (citing the ABS/MBS market has been "about 20% per year"), while adding, "we feel good about our ability to continue to win share." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: asked for an M&A update; Chairman, President & CEO Rodger Levenson said, "no update" and the company sees "a great opportunity to execute on our strategic plan" organically, while noting interest in fee-business opportunities where "the bar would be... very high" for whole-bank deals. (Chairman, President & CEO Levenson)

  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: asked about the large recovery and broader credit concerns; Executive VP & CFO David Burg described the recovered loan as "an acquired loan" tied to "office real estate" where the sponsor refinanced, adding, "there's a lot of liquidity in the market," and said trends across indicators have been "trending down" with "nothing systemic." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative tone around sustainability and sensitivity, with repeated probing on durability and rate/cost offsets, including "overall sustainability" (Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.) and how loan growth should evolve amid payoffs/runoffs (Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen).

  • Management: neutral to slightly positive, leaning on momentum but tempering extrapolation; Executive VP & CFO David Burg said, "would not take this quarter and extrapolate it out" and also, "we are seeing more deposit competition," while still stating, "we do feel like we continue to have momentum." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Versus last quarter: management’s language shifted from the Q4 2025 macro assumption of "three 25 basis point rate cuts" to emphasizing, "Clearly, the March cut didn't happen," and highlighting intensifying pricing pressure: "We are seeing more deposit competition." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4’s full-year baseline, "Our new outlook is now 25 to 35 basis points for the year, down from the previous outlook of 35 to 45 basis points," reflecting the Q1 recovery-driven change, whereas Q4 had set net charge-offs at "35 to 45 basis points of average loans." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg)

  • Rate narrative evolved from Q4’s explicit assumption of "three 25 basis point rate cuts throughout the year" to Q1’s focus on volatility and scenario framing: "we are asset sensitive" and "the March cut didn't happen." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • Analysts maintained recurring themes across both calls—deposit mix/sustainability, Cash Connect/rate impacts, capital return capacity, and credit unevenness—while Q1 added more direct questions on whether deposit growth is "short term and transactional." (Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We are seeing more deposit competition for sure, really across all the businesses." (Executive VP & CFO David Burg) "That pressure is going to continue to be there." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Nonperforming assets... increased linked quarter driven by 2 loans" (a C&I and a multifamily loan), which management said are "well secured." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

  • "Cash Connect fees declined quarter-over-quarter due to the impact of interest rate cuts and lower volumes." (Executive VP & CFO Burg)

Final Takeaway

WSFS management emphasized a Q1 2026 start it described as "strong," anchored by stable "Net interest margin of 3.83%," double-digit fee growth with Wealth & Trust leading, and capital actions that included an 18% dividend increase to $0.20 and an expanded repurchase authorization to 19% of shares. Management also revised its annual net charge-offs outlook to 25 to 35 basis points following the $15.7 million loan recovery, while repeatedly cautioning that deposit pricing pressure is rising across the market and that quarterly deposit growth should not be extrapolated.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Flagstar forecasts adjusted EPS of $0.60-$0.65 for 2026 as CRE payoffs push down net interest income outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flagstar Bank, National Association (FLG) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Joseph Otting said the quarter showed “continued improving fundamentals, strong C&I growth, a high level in growth of core deposits, further progress in reducing the level of nonaccrual and criticized and classified loans, continued margin expansion and industry-leading capital levels,” and emphasized, “We are doing exactly what we set out to do.”
  • Otting highlighted strategic execution on portfolio repositioning, including that multifamily and CRE portfolios “declin[ed] by $1.6 billion or 4% relative to the fourth quarter,” while C&I loan growth marked “the third consecutive quarter.” He also said Fitch and Moody’s upgraded deposit ratings to investment grade and that the bank “disclosed that the previously material weakness in internal controls have been remediated.”
  • CFO Lee Smith said, “We were profitable for the second consecutive quarter,” and pointed to funding-cost progress: “Core deposits, excluding brokered, grew $1.1 billion, and we reduced deposit costs by 21 basis points.”
  • Otting described technology milestones intended to reduce complexity ahead of a 2027 core conversion, noting the bank consolidated “6 legacy data centers into 2 co-location centers,” positioning Flagstar with “ultimately the goal in 2027 is to get on to one core.”

Outlook

  • Smith said 2026–2027 interest income expectations were revised: “We have adjusted our interest income guidance downward for both years as a result of increased CRE and multifamily payoffs, paydowns and amortization,” adding this “reduces interest income and NIM in the short term.”
  • Management said it is retaining fewer resetting loans: “We are currently retaining 35% to 40% of resetting loans versus 50% previously,” which Smith said is expected to be temporary until replaced with new growth.
  • Smith guided size and earnings power, saying, “We expect total assets to be approximately $94 billion at the end of '26 and $102 billion at the end of '27,” and “Adjusted EPS is now forecast to be in the $0.60 to $0.65 range in '26 and in the $1.80 to $1.90 range in '27.”
  • Otting reiterated capital-return sequencing: “We expect the Board taking action on capital distributions in the second half of the year,” and in Q&A added that management wanted “a good insight to that through the second quarter.”

Financial results

  • Smith reported profitability and items affecting comparability: net income attributable to common stockholders was $0.03 per diluted share and “on an adjusted basis” was $0.04; he said the “only one adjustment” was a $9 million decline in the Figure Technologies investment valuation.
  • Net interest margin improved on lower funding costs: Smith said “First quarter NIM increased 10 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 2.15% after adjusting” for a $21 million one-time hedge gain in Q4.
  • Funding mix actions were framed as key drivers: Smith said Flagstar paid off “$1 billion of FHLB advances and $300 million of brokered deposits,” while “operating expenses were again well contained at $441 million.”
  • Credit metrics improved quarter-over-quarter: Smith said nonaccrual loans “declined by $323 million,” criticized and classified loans “decreased by $385 million,” and the allowance for credit losses “decreased $78 million.”
  • Capital remained a focal point: Smith said CET1 ended at 13.24% and that the bank had “about $1.6 billion in excess capital after tax” versus the low end of its target range.

Q&A

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked about margin assumptions; CFO Lee Smith said the NIM reset reflects more CRE/multifamily payoffs, selective retention pricing “in the 1.75% to 2.25% range,” lower reset retention, and deposit mix, “bring the NIM down 10 to 12 basis points.”
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked on capital-return mile markers; CEO Joseph Otting said management is looking for “consistent quarterly earnings,” progress toward “get the nonperforming assets down to $2 billion by the end of the year,” and clarity on C&I growth versus CRE payoffs.
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: asked about loan yields and margin cadence; CEO Otting said Q1 didn’t reflect late-quarter C&I growth, noting “$600 million of that came right at the end of the quarter,” with benefits expected in “Q2 and beyond.”
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: asked about cash/securities; CEO Otting said Flagstar expects to buy “at least $1 billion, $1.5 billion of securities” in Q2 and target securities around “$16 billion or so” in 2H 2026.
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about rating upgrade benefits; CEO Otting said investment-grade deposit ratings help win operating accounts where policies require investment-grade ratings; CFO Smith said, “The upgrades have no direct impact on FDIC expenses.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about core conversion economics; CEO Otting said moving to one core by “July of next year” could be “roughly a $40 million decrease in expenses.”
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked about credit trajectory and surprises; CFO Smith said, “we do not expect any surprises,” and said reductions in criticized/classified should continue, with Q1 charge-offs including a bankruptcy-driven item.
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked about a rent freeze; CFO Smith said the bank modeled a 3-year rent freeze starting “October 1 of '26,” finding no NOI impact at “70% or less rent regulated,” and a “7% or 8%” NOI impact above 70%.
  • David Smith, Truist: asked if guidance cut changes medium-term profitability; CFO Smith said, “worst case is maybe pushes things out 1 quarter or 2 quarters.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor: asked about excess capital pacing; CFO Smith said, “we can do both,” referencing growth and “capital actions later in the year,” and added Basel III analysis could add “60 to 80 basis points of CET1.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor: asked about C&I banker hiring and derisking; CFO Smith said there are “131 customer-facing C&I bankers,” with “another 40 to 60 to go,” and indicated the “tall trees” derisking is “mostly through that.”
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: asked about fee income step-up and NII confidence; CFO Smith pointed to multiple line items and noted “there are more moving parts to this model than probably any other bank out there.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: asked about NPA flows and yield on new loans; CEO Otting said NPA movement has “ins and outs,” and CFO Smith said new C&I loans came on at “242 basis points” spread to SOFR.
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: questioned the 2027 asset path and deposit growth; CFO Smith cited new CRE originations outside NYC and residential mortgage growth, and said deposit momentum can build through new C&I relationships and private bank.
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: asked about deposit pricing and FHLB paydowns; CFO Smith said pricing remains competitive while reducing costs, and expects to pay down “another $2 billion or $3 billion” of FHLB advances in 2026.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on reduced 2026–2027 outlook visibility, the “confidence” in NII, and the pace/timing of capital returns.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly characterizing guidance changes as timing-related; Smith’s phrasing “worst case is maybe pushes things out 1 quarter or 2 quarters” underscored a deferral framing rather than a strategy reset.
  • Versus Q4, management in Q1 used more “pushes” and “moving parts” language around NII/NIM precision, while maintaining a consistent message of execution and credit improvement.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 guidance moved lower: adjusted EPS forecast shifted to $0.60–$0.65 for 2026 and $1.80–$1.90 for 2027, compared with Q4’s $0.65–$0.70 for 2026 and $1.90–$2 for 2027, with management tying the change to faster CRE/multifamily payoffs and fewer reset retentions.
  • Capital strength increased from Q4 to Q1 (CET1 12.83% to 13.24%), while management’s sequencing for capital actions became more time-bound in Q1 with repeated references to the “second half of the year” after “several quarters of sustainable profitability.”
  • The credit narrative stayed consistent quarter-to-quarter—reducing nonaccruals and CRE exposure—but Q1 added explicit rent-freeze scenario modeling and a more detailed 2027 reset-year review process update.

Risks and concerns

  • Smith said elevated CRE/multifamily payoffs and paydowns reduce near-term interest income and NIM, and that fewer resetting loans are being retained; management said selective repricing and replacing runoff with C&I/consumer/new CRE originations are key mitigants.
  • Management highlighted NYC rent-regulated multifamily policy risk, addressing it with modeled stress outcomes and stated reserve/charge-off positioning, while also emphasizing ongoing borrower financial reviews and forward-looking reset analysis.
  • Analysts repeatedly probed the visibility and stability of the NII/NIM outlook, capital deployment timing, and whether guidance reductions could repeat if payoffs stay elevated.

Final takeaway

Management said Q1 2026 reflected accelerating C&I growth, continued deposit cost improvement, shrinking CRE exposure via payoffs, and improving credit metrics, while also resetting the 2026–2027 earnings trajectory lower due to faster CRE/multifamily runoff and lower reset retention. Executives framed the change as a timing shift, emphasized investment-grade deposit rating upgrades as a catalyst for operating account wins, and reiterated that capital distribution discussions are expected to follow additional quarters of profitability and continued nonperforming asset reduction.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Baker Hughes targets $325M Chart synergies while expecting IET orders to exceed $40B by 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Baker Hughes (BKR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Despite a complex operating environment, we delivered another strong quarter of financial results, reflecting the strength of our portfolio and disciplined execution, which more than offset the significant impact of regional disruptions.” (Chairman, President & CEO Lorenzo Simonelli)
  • Simonelli highlighted portfolio actions, saying Baker Hughes “announced the divestiture of Waygate Technologies” and, together with “the sale of PSI to Crane and the joint venture with Cactus,” expects “gross proceeds of approximately $3 billion in 2026.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Simonelli emphasized IET demand and backlog visibility, including “bookings reaching a record of $4.9 billion,” IET “book-to-bill of 1.5x,” and “a record RPO of $33.1 billion,” while also stating, “we are increasingly confident that our Horizon 2 IET order target will exceed $40 billion.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • “As Lorenzo mentioned, we once again delivered strong orders in the first quarter with total company orders of $8.2 billion, including $4.9 billion from IET.” (Executive VP & CFO Ahmed Moghal)

Outlook

  • Moghal guided for Q2 company revenue of $6.5 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $1.13 billion, based on an assumption “the situation in the Middle East will continue through the end of June without further escalation” and that guidance “does not account for any potentially significant secondary impacts, such as elevated inflationary pressures or broader supply chain disruptions.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • For Q2 segments, Moghal projected IET EBITDA of $670 million and OFSE EBITDA of $540 million with OFSE revenues estimated at $3.2 billion, adding that Middle East activity assumptions imply the region “potentially falling… more than 20% sequentially.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • On full-year 2026, Moghal said Baker Hughes is “maintaining our company's revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance range” but “anticipate full year results to be slightly below the midpoint,” while adding, “we believe we are well positioned to achieve at least the $14.5 billion midpoint of our order guidance” for IET. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Compared with Q4’s macro setup that expected “low single-digit declines in global upstream spending in 2026,” Simonelli said Baker Hughes now expects global upstream spending “to be modestly below our prior outlook of low single-digit declines compared to 2025,” driven by “a significant reduction in Middle East activity.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Financial results

  • Baker Hughes reported Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $1.16 billion and adjusted EPS of $0.58; Moghal also reported GAAP diluted EPS of $0.93 and said Q1 adjusted EBITDA margin was 17.6%. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • IET results included revenue of $3.35 billion and EBITDA of $678 million, with margin “expanded by 310 basis points to 20.2%,” while Moghal noted IET revenue was “slightly impacted by shipping delays associated with Middle East disruptions across key trading routes.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • OFSE revenue was $3.24 billion with EBITDA of $565 million and EBITDA margin of 17.4%; Moghal quantified Middle East disruption as impacting OFSE revenue “by approximately 2% when compared to the fourth quarter of 2025.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Moghal said Q1 free cash flow was $210 million and added it was “further affected by some delays in customer payments,” while reporting net debt to adjusted EBITDA declined to 0.32x and cash increased to $14.8 billion after a debt offering intended to fund the Chart acquisition. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked for “intermediate and longer-term impact” of the Middle East conflict on infrastructure spend and redundancy; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli answered that it will “drive fundamental structural change,” including “increased upstream investment,” “rebuilding of global inventories,” and that Baker Hughes “feel good about the opportunity to exceed the $40 billion target for IET orders… in 2028.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Scott Gruber, Citi: Asked why Q2 IET guide looks “more flattish” and how OFSE could change with a quicker Middle East recovery; Executive VP & CFO Moghal said the company assumes the conflict persists through June and “the Strait of Hormuz is not fully operational until we enter the second half of the year,” and noted upside could exist but may be delayed because the larger impact is “on the product sales side… given the logistical challenges.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • John Anderson, Barclays: Asked about Power Systems drivers and data center demand durability; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli said Power Systems booked $1.4 billion of orders across “power generation, grid stability and energy management,” and described behind-the-meter as increasingly “long-term baseload power infrastructure.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Stephen Gengaro, Stifel: Asked whether portfolio optimization is largely done after Waygate and HMH-related proceeds; Executive VP & CFO Moghal said it is “a continuum” but “in the near term, our focus is very much on closing and successfully integrating the Chart transaction.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Saurabh Pant, BofA: Asked if Baker Hughes is capacity constrained in NovaLT and related equipment; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli said, “we're effectively sold out of NovaLTs through 2028,” and that the company is “actively assessing capacity needs across our entire Power Systems portfolio.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing for clarity on Q2 guide softness and Middle East recovery timing, including “the guide is a bit more flattish” and questions on whether OFSE could improve sooner. (Scott Gruber, Citi)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but conditional, repeatedly anchoring to assumptions and uncertainty, including “there's still a great deal of uncertainty” and guidance caveats that exclude “potentially significant secondary impacts.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Versus Q4’s more upbeat macro framing, Q1 introduced a more risk-aware posture tied to conflict impacts, with Simonelli describing “a meaningful new layer of macro uncertainty” and highlighting volatility and disruptions. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 shifted the central macro debate from Q4’s broad “geopolitical and trade-related uncertainty” to a specific Middle East conflict scenario with “effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz,” including commentary that “20% of worldwide LNG capacity now offline.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Capital structure messaging changed, with Q1 detailing a March issuance of “$6.5 billion in U.S. bonds and EUR 3 billion in European bonds” to fund Chart, versus Q4 emphasizing balance sheet strength ahead of closing. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Portfolio actions expanded from Q4’s PSI/SPC completion and $1 billion incremental divestment target to Q1’s Waygate divestiture and expectation of about $3 billion gross proceeds in 2026. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Power Systems discussion remained a major theme in both quarters, but Q1 added explicit capacity constraints and prioritization language such as “sold out… through 2028” and a focus on customers with “financing and offtake firmly in place.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Risks and concerns

  • Simonelli said the Middle East conflict has “introduced significant volatility,” with disruptions affecting “over 10% of global oil volumes,” and cited inflationary pressures as a potential downside if the conflict persists. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Moghal cautioned that Q2 and FY assumptions rely on no further escalation and reopening timelines, and warned the guide excludes “elevated inflationary pressures or broader supply chain disruptions.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • OFSE execution risk was tied to regional declines and logistics, with Moghal describing Middle East product sales pressure due to “equipment imports and exports,” and saying uncertainty “may impact our ability to achieve the midpoint” of OFSE full-year guidance. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)

Final takeaway

Baker Hughes management described Q1 as a strong execution quarter led by record IET orders and expanding IET margins, while framing the Middle East conflict and shipping/logistics disruptions as the primary swing factor for OFSE and near-term guide cadence. Management maintained full-year guidance ranges but positioned results slightly below midpoint and repeatedly anchored Q2 expectations to conflict assumptions through June. Alongside the pending Chart close and a stated $325 million synergy target, management underscored continued portfolio monetization with an expectation of about $3 billion in 2026 gross proceeds and reiterated confidence that IET orders can exceed the $40 billion Horizon 2 target by 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: 25% chance the S&P 500 will hit 8,000 in 2026 – Kalshi
data.description:
Prediction markets Kalshi (KALSHI) indicate that about 26.3% of bettors see the probability that the S&P 500 (SP500) may hit 8,000 or above before the end of the year. 

This bet rose from around 10% at the end of March market trough.

 

 

 

Around 61.2% of investors predict that the S&P 500 (SP500) will rise to 7,600 points or above by the end of the year, and about 44% of them believe it could rise to 7,800 points.

The index has reached new intraday highs on Friday—up about 0.8%—and is on track to end at a record. It has advanced 4.7% year-to-date and about 9.5% in the past month.

S&P 500 ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Calumet cut at Goldman Sachs after shares triple over the past year
data.description:
Calumet (CLMT) down 5.7% in Friday's trading as Goldman Sachs downgraded the small-cap refiner to Neutral from Buy with a $36 price target, following the stock's outperformance over the last 12 months.

After Calumet (CLMT) shares have more than tripled over the past year, Goldman's Neil Mehta said they now better reflect the improving renewable fuels margin backdrop and management's ongoing commitment to balance sheet strength, where the company has addressed near-term maturities.

The analyst said he remains constructive on the outlook for renewable diesel margins and RIN prices, driven by a more supportive 2026-27 RVO ruling and limited production capacity in the U.S., and he continues to see upside to Calumet's (CLMT) 2027 consensus EBITDA estimates and highlights potential upside scenarios.

For investors looking for small/mid cap refining exposure, Mehta highlighted Buy-rated Delek US (DK) as his preferred equity expression of Goldman's more constructive outlook for RIN prices, where he sees 40% upside potential.

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Earnings blitz, FOMC, Pershing Square IPO, and NYC gets a full casino
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - April 27

  • Volatility watch - Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Pitney Bowes (PBI), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Skillz (SKLZ). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Northrop Grumman (NOC), Lockheed (LMT), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). Short interest is elevated on SoundHound AI (SOUN) and BigBear.ai Holdings (BBAI) again.
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Verizon (VZ), Public Storage (PSA), Nucor (NUE), and Domino's Pizza (DPZ). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Rambus (RMBS) after it reports.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Cal-Maine (CALM), AGNC Invest (AGNC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Costco (COST).
  • IPO watch - Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman’s investing vehicle plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Iran watch - Traders will be on edge once again as weekend developments could impact the conflict in the Middle East and energy prices.
  • All day - Shareholders of Calavo Growers (CVGW) will vote on the proposed merger with Mission Produce (AVO).

 

Tuesday - April 28

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Visa (V), Coca-Cola (KO), T-Mobile (TMUS), Booking Holdings (BKNG), Starbucks (SBUX), United Parcel Service (UPS), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), General Motors (GM), Spotify (SPOT), Barclays (BCS), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), and Seagate Technology (STX). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Porch Group (PRCH) and Bloom Energy (BE) after they report.
  • All day - Resorts World is expected to open the first ever casino with live table games in New York City. The Genting Group property will have a monopoly on NYC gaming until Hard Rock International, backed by New York Mets owner Steve Cohen, builds the Metropolitan Park casino resort adjacent to Citi Field in Queens and Bally's Corporation (BALY) develops its site at Ferry Point near the Whitestone Bridge in the Bronx. Caesars Entertainment (CZR) and MGM Resorts (MGM) could see a negative impact at their Atlantic City casinos from the Big Apple casino rollout.
  • All day - AutoNation (AN) will hold its annual general meeting.

 

Wednesday - April 29

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), AbbVie (ABBV), General Dynamics (GD), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Phillips 66 (PSX). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Wingstop (WING), Carvana (CVNA), and Lemonade (LMND) after their reports are released.
  • All day - Uber (UBER) will hold its annual product showcase in New York City.
  • All day - Coca-Cola (KO) will hold its annual meeting.
  • All day - Shareholders of Peakstone (PKST) will vote on the proposed merger with Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).
  • All day - It is the last business day before the application for the Union Pacific (UNP)-Norfolk Southern (NSC) merger needs to be filed with the Surface Transportation Board.
  • 8:30 a.m. The March Durable Goods Orders will be released by the U.S. Census Bureau.
  • 2:00 p.m. The Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision.
  • 2:30 p.m. Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell will hold a press conference.

 

Thursday - April 30

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Apple (AAPL), Eli Lilly (LLY), Mastercard (MA), Caterpillar (CAT), Altria (MO), Amgen (AMGN), ConocoPhillips (COP), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Western Digital (WDC). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Atlassian (TEAM), Wayfair (W), and Reddit (RDDT) after they report.
  • All day - The Federal Reserve is expected to release its statistical report on finance-company receivables, broken into consumer, real estate, and business credit, with levels, flows, and growth rates.
  • All day - FTSE Russell will hold its Rank Day for the 2026 Russell U.S. reconstitution. The index provider ranks all eligible U.S. stocks by total market capitalization at the market close to determine index membership eligibility for the upcoming reconstitution.
  • All day - Roblox's (RBLX) new Plus subscription priced at $4.99 per month begins.
  • All day - The FDA’s Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee meets to review AstraZeneca's (AZN) camizestrant in the morning and Truqap in the afternoon, which could matter for biotech and large-cap pharma sentiment.
  • All day - Shareholders of ZIM Integrated Shipping Services (ZIM) will vote on the proposed merger with Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY). The stock traded 6% below the deal price at the time of publication.
  • All day - Shareholders of Silicon Laboratories (SLAB) will vote on the proposed merger with Texas Instruments (TXN). 
  • 7:30 a.m. The April core PCE price index report will be released. Economists forecast an increase to 3.3% for the core rate.
  • 7:15 a.m. The European Central Bank will release its policy statement.
  • 8:45 a.m. European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde will hold a press conference.

 

Friday - May 1

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Colgate-Palmolive (CL).
  • Data watch - Key data reports due to arrive during the first few days of the week include updates on monthly deliveries from Nio (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI), and BYD Company (BYDDF). Other reports of high interest to investors will be the monthly Macau gross gaming revenue release, the monthly update on Class 8 truck orders, firearm background check data from the FBI, and discount broker DARTs reports.
  • All day - Farmer Brothers (FARM) will hold a special shareholder meeting to vote on the company's merger with Royal Cup.
  • All day - Shareholders of Masimo (MASI) will vote on the proposed merger with Danaher (DHR).
  • All day - Bloomberg will publish its report on oil tanker tracking for April.

 

Saturday - May 2

  • All day - Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) will hold its first annual meeting without Warren Buffett in his usual role.

 

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this report.

data.title: Ricky Sandler's Eminence Capital to close after 27 years of operation - report
data.description:
Ricky Sandler's Eminence Capital is shutting down after 27 years, Bloomberg News reported, citing a letter written by the CEO.

"Over the last few years, it has become increasingly difficult to apply our rigorous bottom-up investment process to rapidly shifting market conditions and an evolving market structure," Sandler said in the letter seen by Bloomberg.

The hedge fund has posted disappointing returns, and expenses have made it difficult to continue the firm, Sandler reportedly wrote.

Eminence Capital is the latest hedge fund to close, reflecting strain on multi-strategy hedge funds.

Recently, AllianceBernstein (AB) reportedly shut its multi-manager fund AB Arya.

data.title: Ford and Geely reportedly discussed joint venture in U.S. -- WSJ
data.description:
Ford (F) reportedly held talks with China’s Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) to extend a potential European joint venture to the U.S., but has walked away as a collaboration would be “politically fraught.”

According to The Wall Street Journal, Ford (F) was in talks to license Geely’s (GELYF) (GELHY) technology in the U.S. as a way to capitalize on more advanced automobile technology while sidestepping a U.S. ban on Chinese tech.

While Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) supports any avenue into the U.S. market, Ford (F) remains outwardly opposed to any threats to the U.S. auto industry.

Ford CEO Jim Farley has warned that while Chinese-made cars are cheaper, better built, and technologically superior, allowing Chinese automakers to sell cars in the U.S. would be “devastating” to the American auto industry.

“Manufacturing is the heart and soul of our country, and for us to lose that to exports would be devastating,” Farley said as recently as this week.

While talks to collaborate in the U.S. have stalled, Ford (F) and Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) continue to discuss possible joint ventures in Europe.

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq on track for another record as U.S. continues ceasefire negotiations with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.7%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.3%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 20.9% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC (TSM), AMD (AMD), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Qualcomm (QCOM).

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

In addition, Trump is sending envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Islamabad this weekend for talks with Iran's foreign minister, according to reports.

On Thursday, Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $94, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $105 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.
